name: Beasley, Edward Bailey
motto: "Plays such fantastic tricks before high Heaven, As make the Angels weep." —Measure for Measure.
description: Given to imitating the beasts of the field and the forest. An ardent biologist, and exponent of the Darwinian theory. Inherits his good taste from his primeval ancestors. His studies have failed to convince him that blue blood is not the purest.

name: Beeuwkes, Henry
motto: "Still falling out with this and this, And finding something still amiss." —Butler. "Applauds to-day what yesterday he cursed." —Sprague.
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
description: His athletic experiences have helped to make him a confirmed kicker. Much given to serio-comic sentimentalism. This is due to an innate feeling that he is an object of persecution, and to an unfortunate political campaign. Apt to change his opinions without notice. A sincere admirer of his brother's essays.

name: Bernheim, Elbridge Palmer
motto: "You may have known that I'm no wordy man." —Otway.
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
Senior Class Delegate, J. H. U. A. A.
description: Leading member of the "Mosquito Fleet" in lacrosse, and a big chief among Gillespie's braves. His duties as class treasurer have cast a slight gloom on his otherwise cheerful countenance. Has a habit of saying dry things, probably in order to get them out of his mouth.

name: Bruner, Lewis Urbin
motto: "A little curly-headed good-for-nothing." —Byron.
description: Bruner is suspected of being a Circassian beauty in disguise, and 'tis rumored that the real color of his fluffy locks is a rich gold. His mysterious silence, and the haste with which he leaves the University as soon as classes are over, tend to confirm these suspicions.

name: Buttner, William Jackins
motto: "Silence does not always mark wisdom."
description: Little known of his true character. Has strong opinions, but refuses to make them public. Occasionally opens his mouth—to yawn. His ambition is to get a scholarship at Gallaudet.

name: Cameron, Phineas Jenks Smith
motto: "I cannot tell what the dickens his name is."—Shakspere. "I to myself am dearer than a friend."
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
description: A typical college man, (in his own estimation). Addicted to flights of questionable poetry. A skillful cutter, but was once caught napping. His voice is even louder than his dress.

name: Campbell, John Gorsuch
motto: "His speech was a fine sample, on the whole, Of rhetoric which the learn'd call 'rigmarole'." —Byron.
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
Freshman, Junior and Senior Class Executive Committees
Senior Class Delegate, J. H. U. A. A.
President of the Society for the Preservation of Dignity
description: Is able to detect the serious side of an absurdity. Speaks in sections at great length, with but little breadth. A man of rigid morals whose baseball career is marred by conscientious scruples against stealing bases.

name: Alberta Goucher Dew
motto:
"A man whose blood
Is very snow-broth."
—Shakspere.
description: An early acquaintance with the sins of others has saddened both his countenance and his garb. He has loved to learn, and learned to love. A good student, but a poor comrade.

name: Ross Miles Diggs
motto:
"They never taste who always drink,
They always talk who never think."
—Prior.
description: Affects an aesthetic grace, which is chiefly due to a flowing Latin Quarter tie. Exhibits marvellous dignity in coming late to class. Is the most consummately skillful loafer in the University. Has an innate idea that he is of some importance. The Board can vouch for the entire originality of this conception.

name: England, Joseph Townsend
motto: "Pleased with a rattle, tickled with a straw." —Pope.
clubs: Member Relay Team making record for the South, '01
Junior Class Delegate J. H. U. A. A.
description: Young and foolish. Like many good children, he often tries to be bad. Much admired by the tellers of poor jokes, as he laughs at everything. Is known to lead a "fast" life.

name: Gillespie, Horace Clendenin
motto: "I profess not talking, only this. Let each man do his best." —Shakspere.
clubs: Captain, Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
Junior and Senior Class Executive Committees
Junior Class Delegate, J. H. U. A. A.
Treasurer, J. H. U. A. A., '01-'02
description: A steady old war-horse. Enjoys life in a quiet way, except in a football game, where he is always very much in evidence. Has an enigmatic smile that would squelch a professional interviewer.

name: Guggenheimer, Frederick Lee
motto: "He coude songes make, and wel endite." —Chaucer.
clubs: Secretary and Speaker, Senate Debating Team
description: A convicted poet, but otherwise not criminal. Thinks he has mastered Herbert Spencer's philosophy, and has concluded that Spencer agrees with him. Practices cynical and blasé expressions before the looking-glass daily, and has succeeded only in seeming stiff and conceited. But those who can sound the depths of his mind say he's a good fellow.

name: Hand, John Phelps
motto: "Wide is his parishe, and houses fer asonder." —Chaucer.
description: Fond of over-awing ignorant lower classmen. Always looks on the bright side of things, and is much loved by the professors who tell jokes. A person who teaches his fellow-men how to go to Heaven—or elsewhere—with a laughing face.

name: Hazen, Henry Honeyman
motto: "Seldom he smiles; and smiles in such a sort, As if he mocked himself, and scorn'd his spirit, That could be moved to smile at anything." —Shakspere.
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
description: Of a belligerent and morose disposition, which showed itself, while he was still young, in his participation in a secret conspiracy. Later he withdrew from the "madding crowd," and delved deeply into the psychological mysteries of the Dean's course. Smiling is no laughing matter with Hazen.

name: Hildebrandt, Louis Foltat
motto: "Now as thou lov'st me, do him not that wrong, To bear a hard opinion of his truth." —Shakspere.
description: He has seen much of the wickedness of the world, and can now smoke his pipe with the air of a philosopher. Cracks a small joke with a sledge-hammer. Does not believe in spirits, especially class-spirit. Has written an essay on "The Similarity in Appearance and Structure of Ancient and Modern Exam-Papers."

name: Gton, D. C.
motto: "All smiles, and bows, and courtesy was he." —Watson.
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Executive Committees
description: Formerly a member of a political band whose music was out of harmony with the times. Has now ceased wire-pulling, and is content to pluck banjo strings. His disappointments have not interfered with his enjoyment of life.

name: Hill, William Bryant
motto: "Let but the puppets move, I've my desire, Unseen the hand that guides the master wire." —Churchill.
clubs: Corresponding Secretary, J. H. U. A. A., '00-'01, '01-'02
description: A man who never hesitates to use the powers of office. Surrounded by an atmosphere which blights all sentiment except political affection. Considers "The Burgomaster" the ideal of musical composition. His experiences are interesting, and he will relate them upon application.

name: Hoffman, William Edwin
motto: "Stabbed with a white wench's black eye." —Shakspere.
clubs: Member of Relay Team, making record for the South, '01
description: Silence and a smile. Fond of the girls, and tolerates the boys. Not inclined to waste his valuable words on his fellow-students. Proof of the modern adage that he who runs may not read.

name: Kirk, William
motto: "What a good boy am I."
clubs: Speaker pro. tem., House of Representatives
description: The laughing philosopher; sometimes called Billy by irreverent classmen. Fond of argument but generally speaks on both sides of a question. Feels that he does very wicked things, but tries to expiate his wickedness by converting others.

name: Koppleman, William Howard
motto: "A mere court butterfly That flutters in the pageant of a monarch." —Byron.
description: On account of his youth and inexperience he is astonished at all he hears. His eyes have a wistful questioning look. Likes to hear criticisms of the faculty. Is somewhat depressed as a result of his having attempted to enter politics at too early an age.

name: Macht, David Israel
motto: "The greatest clerkes ben not the wisest men." —Chaucer.
description: Full of knowledge, but never spills any. Not recognizing the existence of mankind he is not tied down by social conventionalities. Attends class meetings out of purely abstract considerations.

name: Ogle Marbury
motto: "The fashion Doth wear out more apparel than the man." —Shakespeare.
description: Tries to dress in the latest style in order to conceal the fact that he is from the country. A critic, whose criticism is more original than valuable. Is possessed by the idea that he is more of a genius than people think. Often laughs convulsively for no apparent reason.

name: Charles Markell, Jr.
motto: "I'll speak, * * * though hell itself should gape And bid me hold my peace." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Secretary and Speaker, House Debating Team
Chairman and Speaker, Senate Debating Team
description: Can argue round a point, but never hits it. Is affectionately disposed towards his ideas, and reluctantly parts with them. Expects to be long-lived, and therefore does not hesitate to use a plethora of words. On the roof of the cage he can sing a jolly song, in a far from jolly way.

name: Bartgis Mcglone
motto: "A most discordant store of words he foolishly poured out. * * *" "Anything with which he could procure laughter, he never could contain." —Chapman's "Iliad."
description: A laughing child ignorant of the world's cares. On the geological excursion he was found playing with his hammer, in great glee. At the Y. M. C. A. he shows no fear of Satan, but considers him a jolly bugaboo. Exams may come, exams may go, McGlone plays on forever.

name: Robert Emory Moore
motto: "A great man is not always a wise man."
clubs: Freshman and Junior Class Football Teams
description: Once had ambitions, but their fire was quickly quenched. Inventor of a short-hand system of performing experiments in physics. Has some class spirit, which rises to the surface whenever there is any hazing going on. His body is stronger than his will.

name: McHardy Mower, K. A.
motto: "Whoever is delighted in solitude is either a wild beast or a god."—Plato.

name: Lyman Clements Murphy, Φ. Γ. Δ.
motto: "Full well they laughed with counterfeited glee, at all his jokes, for many a joke had he." —Goldsmith.
description: Like most Frenchmen, Pat is rather witty. Has a stock of jokes which he will relate upon the slightest provocation. Author of a monograph on "The Dialect of the Drunkard." Pat's ideal of earthly felicity is the life of a sailor with a wife in every port. NEWBERRY, S. C. Has been known to speak six times since entering the University, but no record has been kept, and the data are now lost in the tradition of antiquity.

name: Myers, Charles Augustus
motto: "When I am here, a quorum is present."
description: A cautious man, slow to anger and to action, who never begins a statement without "Well, I don't know about that." When not studying he may be seen at the local theaters, or walking the streets with anyone who will listen to the tales of his troubles as Czar of the "HULLABALOO." A grandson of Job.

name: Myers, John Norris
motto: "And spoke the simplest facts emphatically."
description: A very noisy young person, given to expressing his opinions in a vehement and forcible manner. His speech is by no means of the yea! yea! and nay! nay! order. Possesses quite a stock of poetry which has never been printed.

name: Ogle, Marbury Bladen
motto: "Man, being reasonable, must get drunk."
description: A hard student of classics and anatomy. Famous for the introduction of tobacco and blue language into the Classical department. Has in project an immense undertaking—the modernization of the oaths used by Homer.

name: Pearre, Aubrey
motto: "I pray thee cease thy counsel."
clubs: Chairman, Committee of Arrangements, Inter-Class Debate, '01-'02
Chairman, Committee of Arrangements Inter-Collegiate Debate, '01-'02
description: A great manager, frequently overwhelmed by the weight of his own dignities. Affects a business-like air, and is rather prone to give advice. Author of a drama, in many acts, "The Private Secretary." Chews the cud of his own actions.

name: Isaac Rosenbaum Pels
motto: "There's such a charm in melancholy, I would not, if I could, be gay." —Rogers.
description: A man of soft speech and gentle manner, who thinks little and talks less. The inspiration of his genius is work. He knows the latest sayings, and although a dreamer, he is able to appreciate a properly explained joke.

name: Thomas Coulson Pinkerton
motto: "To kneel at many a shrine, Yet lay the heart on none." —Moore.
description: Very retiring in appearance. Speaks in a low, subdued tone, which leads the unsuspecting to regard him as a religious reformer. Extremely fond of society, in the shape of small and select supper-parties.

name: Putts, Benjamin Swayne
motto: "Facts, hard facts."
description: A stolid business man, devoid of brilliance save in lacrosse. Of few words and plain. Has the elements of a great confidence man, as his success as class treasurer indicates. Suspected of social activity on the side.

name: Ranft, Charles Frederick
motto: "The lunatic, the lover, and the poet, Are of imagination all compact." —Shakspere.
description: A grand-stand player at lacrosse and everything else. His popularity with the fair sex has served to increase the sentimentality of his verse. He has not yet decided whether to become a great poet or a great artist. His indecision is not the only obstacle to his becoming either.

name: Reynolds, Walter Ford
motto: "As cold as cucumbers." —Beaumont and Fletcher.
description: A cold mathematician who still retains a propensity for convulsive giggling. Fond of relating his experiences in the physical laboratory. As he already has an absent air, and absent hair, he will be a glory to his department.

name: Schmeisser, William Christian
motto: "As headstrong as an allegory on the banks of the Nile." —Sheridan.
description: The greatest man in the class, in the opinion of the captain of this year's lacrosse team. A man who conceives remarkable but by no means valuable ideas, and holds them with all the tenacity of paternal fondness. Stares at an idea until he is hypnotized by it, in which condition he mistrusts everything.

name: Sharretts, Ralph Carleton
motto: "Friend Ralph, thou hast Outrun the constable at last."
description: Little given to anything useful, but inclined to spend his time in drawing risqué creations of his fancy. When he falls asleep in the trophy-room, he may be heard to mutter, "My art and my moustache, my moustache and my art—voilà tout."

name: Sisco, Gibson Emerson
motto: "I never thrust my nose into other men's porridge."—Cervantes.
description: Possesses a remarkable disinclination to do anything at all. Is up to all the latest labor-saving devices.

name: Slemons, John Aydelotte
motto: "The big round dumpling rolling from the pot."—Humphreys. "Still amorous and fond and billing."—Butler.
clubs: Assistant Manager 'Varsity Lacrosse Team '00-'01, and Manager '01-'02
description: There is a great disproportion between his corporeality and his spirituality, and it is not the latter which is the larger. Dotes on the fleshpots of Egypt and other palmy regions. A man of great weight but little influence.

name: Smith, Sidney
motto: "The mildest-mannered man that ever scuttled ship, or cut a throat."—Byron.
description: Became most famous when he least desired it. The acquisition of fame was so sudden that he seems to have been dazed by it. Many pipes of tobacco have not sufficed to restore his equanimity.

name: Stone, Harvey Brinton
motto: "Men, like bullets, go farthest when they are smoothest." —Richter.
description: Will listen to any one; trusts only himself. Is really able to sympathize equally with two opponents. In his attempts to reconcile opposing claims, he has acquired that puzzled air which distinguishes him while thinking. Resides "on the fence" when people are looking. At present it is difficult to decide whether he or Crane will become Senator Gorman's successor.

name: Strouse, Elliott
motto: "O how full of briars is this working-day world." —Shakspere.
clubs: Freshman Class Delegate, J. H. U. A. A.
description: Attends prize-fights to get pointers for baseball. His enthusiasm for the national game is so great that he has not enough left to join in the rooting at other games. Hides his true character under a cynical exterior.

name: Solomon Strouse
motto:
"They who have light in themselves will not revolve as satellites."
                                                                             —Anon.
description: Of an analytic and introspective nature. Has ideals, and sometimes feels that these have been realized. Later his opinion changes. Calls the attention of his friends to the faults which he discovers in their characters. Tries to be as much like the President as is consistent with the dignity of the Vice-President.

name: Samuel Wolman
motto:
"For I am nothing if not critical."
                                                                           —Shakspere.
description: Unworldly, unpractical, abstract, yet consistently analytic and logical. Marked by critical sarcasm, and famous for his continuous performances with Markell, the Dean, and the Senate. The ministry assert that he is a sub-rosa member of the "reds."

name: Peregrine Wroth, Jr.
motto: "' Give me a theme, the little poet cried / And I will do my part.'" —Gilder.
description: A poet fallen on evil days, condemned by fate to mourn the misfortunes of those whose triumphs he was to sing. Now sadly moralizes upon the mutability of fortune.
